title: Food-safe wood finishes compared
subtitle: Raw linseed, walnut oil, tung oil, and board butter: which to choose and when
excerpt: Not every oil finish is food-safe, and not every food-safe finish suits every project. This guide explains the four main food-safe options for carved treen, cutting boards, and spoons, covering cure time, durability, re-coat schedule, and the allergen considerations that apply to walnut and tung.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for traditional treen finishes and drying-oil chemistry. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for notes on oiling wooden kitchen utensils.

glossaryTerms.definition: An oil that cures to a solid film through oxidative polymerisation rather than evaporation. Linseed, walnut, and tung oil are all drying oils. Mineral oil and olive oil are not and will eventually turn rancid on wood.
Small wooden objects made by hand, typically for household use: spoons, bowls, cups, scoops, and similar domestic pieces. The word is Old English for wooden things.

content.text: A carved spoon or bowl that will touch food needs a finish that is food-safe once set, that protects the wood from moisture and bacterial colonisation of the grain, and that can be refreshed at home without specialist materials. The options for
narrow quickly when the food-safe constraint is applied. The four main choices are raw linseed oil, walnut oil, pure tung oil, and board butter.

attrs.title: Boiled linseed oil is not food-safe
attrs.body: Boiled linseed oil contains metallic driers and is not food-safe; use raw linseed oil only.

content.text: pressed from the seed of the flax plant. It is the traditional finish for carved wooden kitchen objects across northern Europe and has been used continuously since at least the medieval period. The finish hardens through oxidative polymerisation: oxygen from the air reacts with the fatty acid chains in the oil to form a cross-linked polymer network within the wood fibres. The process is slow at room temperature.

Application: apply liberally with a clean cloth, working the oil into the grain. Leave for one hour so the oil soaks in. Wipe off every trace of surface oil with a dry cloth. A spoon finished this way will look barely changed: there is very little surface film. Allow to cure for two weeks in a warm, ventilated space before food contact. Re-oil whenever the wood looks or feels dry, typically every three to six months for a spoon in regular use.

Limitations: the drying time is two weeks. A spoon oiled on a Sunday is not food-safe by the following Sunday. A warm, well-ventilated space speeds the cure; a damp, cold shed slows it. Raw linseed builds no surface film and the protection it provides is light compared to a film finish, which is why it needs periodic re-application.

Walnut oil is another drying oil and it hardens more fully and faster than raw linseed. The cure time is around one week in warm conditions. The finished surface is slightly harder and less prone to going tacky in summer. Application is the same: oil, soak, wipe, cure.

attrs.title: Tree-nut allergen
attrs.body: Walnut oil is pressed from walnuts; declare the finish if making for someone with tree-nut allergies.

content.text: Pure tung oil, pressed from the seed of the tung tree, is the hardest-curing of the three drying oils. Three to four weeks at room temperature for full hardness. The cured surface is noticeably more durable than raw linseed and holds up well to the repeated wash cycles that a daily-use spoon or chopping board receives. For a spoon intended for long-term daily use, tung oil is worth the longer cure wait.

Apply in three thin coats rather than one heavy coat. The first coat soaks into bare wood and is absorbed almost entirely. The second coat adds the main layer. The third coat fills the grain to a low satin. Allow each coat to penetrate for thirty minutes and then wipe off the surface before the next coat goes on. Allow a full day between coats.

Board butter is a paste of beeswax and food-grade mineral oil. It does not cure in the way a drying oil hardens; it is a barrier-and-conditioner that sits in the surface of the wood rather than polymerising into it. This means it needs refreshing more often, typically monthly for a chopping board in regular use. It also means it is ready to use immediately after application: there is no drying time.

Application: rub a generous amount of board butter into the wood with a soft cloth, leave for twenty minutes, then buff off with a clean dry cloth. The surface should be left dry to the touch, not greasy. Board butter is the most accessible finish for a new maker and the easiest to maintain. It does not provide the same degree of water resistance as a cured drying oil and is not the right choice for the exterior of a willow basket or any outdoor piece, but for indoor kitchen treen it is practical and non-toxic.

All food-safe oil-finished wood follows the same care rule: hand wash in warm water and mild soap, then dry immediately with a towel. Never soak in water and never put in a dishwasher. Long water contact raises the grain, opens the surface, and pushes the oil out of the wood, shortening the time between re-applications. A spoon that goes in the dishwasher once may need re-oiling immediately and will age faster than one that is always hand-washed.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Finish problems that come up after the first season of use.

items.symptom: The spoon smells of oil even weeks after finishing
items.cause: The surface oil was not wiped off after application, leaving an uncured excess on the surface rather than inside the wood.
items.fix: Wipe the surface clean with a dry cloth and leave in a warm, ventilated spot for a further week. Future applications: always wipe off every trace of surface oil.

items.symptom: The wood surface feels rough and raised after washing
items.cause: The grain has been raised by water contact. This happens most often in the early life of a piece before the oil has fully penetrated and cured.
items.fix: Allow to dry thoroughly, then re-oil and re-sand very lightly with 400-grit sandpaper if the surface is still rough after oiling. Raised grain reduces with each oil cycle as the pores fill.

items.symptom: The finish looks patchy with some areas darker and some lighter after oiling
items.cause: The wood has variable porosity across the surface, typical of figured or end-grain sections.
items.fix: Apply a second coat after the first has soaked in for thirty minutes, concentrating on the paler areas. This is normal on a first-finishing cycle and evens out with subsequent coats.
